pattern: Precomputing each node's 2nd, 4th, 8th, ... ancestor up front turns any single ancestor query into a handful of table lookups picked out by the binary digits of k.
hints: Walking up one parent link at a time per query is too slow when there can be tens of thousands of queries on a tree with tens of thousands of nodes.
Precompute, for every node, its ancestor 2^0, 2^1, 2^2, ... steps up (each row built from the previous one, since the 2^j-th ancestor is the 2^(j-1)-th ancestor of the 2^(j-1)-th ancestor).
To answer getKthAncestor, consume k one bit at a time: whenever a bit is set, jump using that power-of-two row; stop early and return -1 the moment you jump off the top of the tree.

edge_inputs.description: a small branching tree with several ancestor depths, including one query past the root
a straight chain, walking multiple steps up including exactly to the root
a star topology where every non-root node is a direct child of the root
a single-node tree: k=0 returns the node itself, k=1 has no ancestor
k=0 must return the queried node itself, not its parent
requesting an ancestor exactly one step beyond the root must give -1, not wrap around

stress.description: a random tree near the max node count with a long stream of random ancestor queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 50000))
    parent = [-1] + [rng.randint(0, i) for i in range(n - 1)]
    ops = ['TreeAncestor']
    args = [[n, parent]]
    for _ in range(min(50000, max(1, n))):
        ops.append('getKthAncestor')
        args.append([rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(0, n)])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: parent[i] < i for i > 0 keeps it a valid tree rooted at 0

stress.description: a single long chain, stressing deep ancestor jumps
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 50000))
    parent = [-1] + list(range(n - 1))
    ops = ['TreeAncestor']
    args = [[n, parent]]
    for _ in range(2000):
        ops.append('getKthAncestor')
        args.append([rng.randint(0, n - 1), rng.randint(0, n)])
    return [ops, args]

complexity.time: O(n log n) build, O(log n) per query
